step2_rag_chunks: Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

- Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.
- Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
- Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

step2_rag_chunks: Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

Triggers are situations or emotions that make someone want to smoke. Common triggers include stress, drinking alcohol, social gatherings, and boredom.

step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready. That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.

step2_rag_chunks: If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.
